component_identification.content: Dominion (base/2nd edition line) includes 500 cards organized into base treasures/victory/curses, 25 kingdom sets (10 copies each), randomizers/blanks, and a Trash card. Core base piles: Copper (60), Silver (40), Gold (30), Estate (24), Duchy (12), Province (12), and Curse (30) for typical 2–4p setup, with supply counts adjusted by player count where applicable in official rules. Each player starts with a 10-card deck of 7 Copper and 3 Estate. The play area uses Supply piles, a Trash area, and each player’s deck/discard/hand/in-play zones.

core_game_loop.content: A turn has three phases: Action, Buy, and Clean-up. In Action phase, you may play one Action card unless effects grant extra actions. In Buy phase, you play treasures and spend coins to buy one card unless effects grant extra buys; bought cards go to discard by default. In Clean-up, discard hand and played cards, then draw 5 new cards. Players continue alternating turns until an end-game trigger is met, then total victory points in full decks.

detailed_rules.content: Game setup always uses base treasure/victory/curse piles plus exactly 10 selected kingdom piles. Card text is authoritative and can modify turn structure, costs, gains, and interactions. If a deck empties, reshuffle discard to form a new deck when drawing is required. Gaining is distinct from buying: effects can gain cards without using buys. Trashing removes cards from your deck ecosystem permanently and is a key engine concept. Attacks affect opponents unless prevented by reaction text or immunity granted by specific cards. Resolve instructions in printed order, fully completing one card before moving to another unless a card explicitly interrupts timing. Costs are in coins unless text references debt/potion in expansion contexts (ignore if not in chosen setup). End-game triggers in base rules: Province pile empty OR any three supply piles empty; game ends after current player’s turn. Common misplays: putting gained cards into hand without explicit text, taking extra buys without source, and ending game immediately mid-turn before cleanup resolution. Teaching clarifications from rulebook-first play: always resolve effects in printed timing windows and finish mandatory steps before optional effects. If a card or tile text conflicts with a baseline rule, the specific card text takes priority for that interaction only. During teaching, call out hidden information boundaries, legal action checks before payment, and tie handling exactly as written. Most play disputes come from skipped sequence steps, so keep turn order visible and confirm each phase aloud in early rounds.

scoring_and_endgame.content: After the game ends, each player counts all victory points in deck, discard, hand, and in-play cards. Base values: Estate 1, Duchy 3, Province 6, Curse -1, plus any points from kingdom cards used in that setup. Highest VP wins. Tie-breaker goes to player with fewer turns (because current player may have had one extra turn); if still tied, shared win. Dominion rewards engine timing: deck strength during play matters only insofar as it converts into more VP by game end.

beginner_strategy.content: Open with economy and deck quality, not early green cards. Buying too many victory cards too soon reduces draw consistency and stalls your engine. Prioritize one reliable payload plan (money density, draw-based turns, village/action chains, or strong gainers) based on the available kingdom set. Trashing weak starters (especially Estates) is often the highest-value early improvement. Watch pile pressure every turn: if three piles are nearing empty, pivot from long-term engine upgrades to immediate points or pile control. Learn to count buy power distribution in your deck so you know when to green. Also draft with interaction in mind—if attacks are present, include defense or speed to avoid losing tempo. New-player coaching tip: choose one clear plan by the end of early game, then spend midgame turns increasing action efficiency rather than collecting disconnected benefits. Track remaining rounds/end triggers constantly; many losses come from mis-timing rather than bad tactical choices. When uncertain between two similar options, prefer the move that preserves flexibility for the next turn while still scoring now.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text as primary reference.
